bullets.text: Fragile quiet in Lebanon as US-Iran truce leaves unanswered questions, with many Lebanese remaining doubtful that the agreement could finally mean the end of the fighting between Israel and Hezbollah.
Violent attacks on schools, pupils and staff around the world have increased by 40%, with cases reported in 83 countries and at least 10,600 students and staff killed, injured, abducted, or arrested.
Global brands including Amazon and Sony may have sourced coltan, a mineral used in phones, from supply chains controlled by M23 rebels accused of atrocities in the Democratic Republic of Congo.
Police are investigating two suspicious fires at a South Melbourne gym, which was targeted by arsonists two days in a row.
The Queensland government has set a target to more than double the household recycling rate from 28 per cent to 60 per cent, aiming for a 65 per cent recycling target as waste outstrips growth.
One Nation's health policies have been criticized by experts, who warn they are based on misconceptions and could incur costs for taxpayers.
US and European officials are discussing a 'trusted partner' scheme to allow allies to test cutting-edge AI models, following a dispute involving Anthropic.
SpaceX has become the world's fifth-most valuable company, surpassing Amazon, and announced a $60 billion deal for the AI coding app Cursor.
The default rate among private-credit borrowers has reached a new high in a KBRA index, indicating increasing stress within the $1.8 trillion private credit market.
